title: Clarify inventory-only command versus full repo workflow
description: External clone validation showed a new user can reasonably run seal-inventory and then proof/launch commands from the README command inventory, but seal-inventory only writes MAP/views/gap review and does not create proof/evidence artifacts. Clarify that seal-invoke is the complete starter command for plan or repository workflows, while seal-inventory is a repository map refresh command for workspaces that already have or separately manage the rest of the .seal artifact set. Rerun external clone validation with the documented full workflow.
close_reason: README now distinguishes seal-invoke as the complete starter workflow from inventory-only maintenance; local test and smoke gates pass.

title: Add right-sized rigor profiles and question routing
description:
## Background
SEAL needs risk-tailored rigor: lightweight for discovery, conservative for launch, and stricter when the user/artifacts declare high consequence. This mirrors systems-engineering tailoring: adjust process to objectives, allowable risk, and constraints without making hidden assumptions.

## Profiles
Implement these canonical profiles:
- `explore`: discovery/planning. Minimal required artifacts, permissive warnings, no launch recommendation, all missing authority/unknowns visible.
- `standard`: default repo/product work. Requires valid MAP/PLAN/SOURCES/TRACE/DEBT/proof plan, intact refs, file coverage or ownership gaps, and normal validation.
- `launch`: release/closeout work. Requires IMPACT, PROVE, evidence index, readiness report, pending approvals visible, failed evidence blocking, `seal validate`, closure evidence for closed P0/P1 beads when applicable.
- `mission-critical`: user/artifact-declared high consequence. Requires explicit source authority, approval state, proof obligations for safety/security/reliability/data/cost claims, unresolved gaps carried as blockers or accepted assumptions, and no claim that safety is proven unless evidence says so.

## Implementation Scope
1. Add a profile policy model, preferably near `src/gates/*` or a dedicated `src/rigor/*`, with profile-to-requirement mappings.
2. Add CLI/plugin routing so `seal guide` and readiness/proof outputs can show/use the selected profile, with `standard` as default.
3. Extend `QUESTION_DECISION_TREE` behavior with profile-aware questions for missing intent, missing authority, conflicting sources, approvals, high-risk gaps, and launch intent.
4. Feed profile requirements into validation/readiness/gate reporting without weakening the existing gate criteria.
5. Document profile selection and examples for software and non-software/product-system use cases.

## Product Decisions
- Profile names are approved as `explore`, `standard`, `launch`, and `mission-critical`.
- `mission-critical` remains opt-in only. SEAL may detect high-consequence signals and recommend escalation, show a blocker/warning, or run/request a mission-critical review helper when explicitly configured, but it must not silently switch the canonical profile from user/artifact intent.
- Any auto-escalation must be visible in output, record why it happened, and preserve source authority/unknowns instead of claiming safety or compliance.

## Research Anchors
- Matt Pocock's writing-great-skills guidance favors predictable invocation, explicit user-vs-model invocation boundaries, and context loading that does not surprise the user.
- Systems-engineering tailoring supports scaling rigor to risk while keeping authority and assumptions explicit.

## Test Plan
Add policy matrix tests, CLI/profile parsing tests, question-routing tests, readiness/gate regression tests, fixture workflows at `explore`, `standard`, `launch`, and `mission-critical`, auto-escalation/recommendation tests, plus `npm test`.
acceptance_criteria: Profiles explore, standard, launch, and mission-critical are implemented as explicit policy mappings used by guide/readiness/gate outputs; they adjust prompts, required artifacts, evidence, approvals, launch gates, and visible escalation recommendations proportionally while preserving visible unknowns, source authority, failed/stale evidence, accepted gaps, and the rule that mission-critical is opt-in unless declared by the user/artifacts or enabled by an explicit configuration/flag.
notes: 2026-07-01 product decision: profile names accepted. Mission-critical stays opt-in, but implementation may add visible high-consequence escalation recommendations or explicit opt-in auto-escalation/review behavior. Independent skill research from mattpocock/skills reinforces keeping invocation boundaries predictable and user intent explicit.
close_reason: Implemented explicit rigor profiles across guide, gate, proof, and readiness outputs with docs and closure coverage; npm test, npm run test:closure, npm run smoke:plugin, and bd dep cycles passed

title: Audit SEAL as a composable Codex skill
description:
## Background
SEAL is effectively a large Codex skill plus a local CLI. The product layer should be audited using Matt Pocock's `writing-great-skills` guidance so invocation, context loading, command routing, and generated documentation remain predictable instead of sprawling.

## Research Source
Use the downloaded `mattpocock/skills` repository, `writing-great-skills`, commit `b38badf7091afc614dedffc03ea8c8ad2b643cb4`, and record the source URL `https://github.com/mattpocock/skills` in the audit report.

## Audit Scope
Inspect the full SEAL skill/product surface:
1. Plugin skills: `plugin/skills/**/SKILL.md`.
2. CLI router and public commands, especially `src/cli/seal.mjs`.
3. README/plugin docs and generated-doc boundaries.
4. Product beads and workflow docs that shape user/model invocation.
5. Human docs vs AI/machine docs separation.

## Audit Criteria
Evaluate and record findings for:
- Predictability: same trigger should load the same process and complete at the right boundary.
- Invocation mode: user-invoked vs model-invoked skills are explicit; mission-critical and high-rigor flows do not surprise users.
- Descriptions: leading words, one trigger branch per description branch, no duplicated identity text.
- Information hierarchy: SKILL.md holds the active process, references are disclosed behind context pointers, branch-only details are not always loaded.
- Split decisions: split by invocation or sequence when it prevents premature completion or cognitive overload.
- Pruning: remove no-op, duplicate, stale, sedimented, or sprawling instructions.
- Traceability: skill guidance and generated outputs point back to canonical SEAL artifacts or visible gaps.

## Output
Produce `.seal/reports/skill-quality-audit.md` as a generated/non-authoritative audit report, and file/update bd beads for any implementation findings that should block product completion.

## Test Plan
This bead may be report-only, but the audit must name the inspected paths and source commit. Any code/doc changes proposed or made from findings must include appropriate tests/gates, at minimum `npm test`, `npm run smoke:plugin`, and `bd dep cycles` when implemented.
acceptance_criteria: A generated `.seal/reports/skill-quality-audit.md` audits SEAL against Matt Pocock writing-great-skills guidance at commit `b38badf7091afc614dedffc03ea8c8ad2b643cb4`, covers every plugin skill plus CLI/docs invocation surface, records pass/fail/findings for invocation, information hierarchy, descriptions, split/pruning decisions, human-vs-AI docs separation, and traceability, and converts blocking findings into bd beads or explicit acceptance criteria before product-layer closeout.
close_reason: Produced generated/non-authoritative skill-quality audit report against mattpocock/skills writing-great-skills commit b38badf7091afc614dedffc03ea8c8ad2b643cb4, covered plugin skills plus CLI/docs/product beads, and added machine-checkable audit coverage; node tests/skill-quality-audit-report.test.mjs, npm test, npm run test:closure, npm run smoke:plugin, and bd dep cycles passed

title: Add SEAL readiness levels after core gates
description:
## Background
TRL or SRL-style levels can help users understand maturity, but only after the base gates and evidence model are working.

## Technical Approach
Define lightweight readiness levels that summarize artifact completeness, repo map coverage, proof strength, and launch gate state.

## Success Criteria
Readiness levels provide a useful maturity summary without replacing concrete gate failures or proof gaps.

## Test Plan
Score fixture projects at several maturity levels and confirm the score matches the underlying gate evidence.

## Considerations
Keep readiness levels secondary to the actual evidence-backed gate report.
close_reason: Added SEAL Readiness Levels to launch reports, including SRL-0 through SRL-5 scoring tied to gate policy, map coverage, impact scope, and proof strength; documented the levels and covered pass/warn/blocked/fail maturity fixtures.

title: RC command regression assumed fixed impact filename
description: Observed while validating bead seal-zfm: node tests\\rc-command-surface.test.mjs failed after seal impact because the regression asserted .seal/impacts/IMPACT-src-index-js.yaml, but the CLI-generated impact filename is not guaranteed to be that exact path. The test should verify the impact command creates a valid IMPACT artifact without encoding an unpromised filename.
close_reason: Updated the RC command regression to verify generated impact artifacts without assuming an unpromised fixed filename.

title: Update example workflow docs contract for short RC commands
description: Observed while running npm test for seal-zfm: tests/example-workflows-docs.test.mjs still requires the old node src/cli/seal-invoke.mjs example after the public RC workflow moved to npm exec -- seal repo map / plan ingest / impact / proof / launch / validate. Update the docs contract to verify the short command surface instead of the legacy invocation path.
close_reason: Updated example workflow docs and docs tests to use the short public RC command surface.

title: Create unmapped and hidden-debt register
description:
## Background
The safe promise is zero hidden technical debt, not zero technical debt. SEAL needs a visible register for gaps, orphan components, missing tests, stale artifacts, risky dependencies, and ambiguous ownership.

## Technical Approach
Write a structured register that records unmapped files, unknown owners, unlinked tests, missing evidence, stale artifacts, orphan components, risky dependencies, and ambiguous requirements with source refs and confidence.

## Success Criteria
Every unmapped or uncertain repo finding is either resolved into the map or listed as a visible debt/unknown record with a reason and next action.

## Test Plan
Run fixture repos with intentional orphan files, missing tests, stale docs, and ambiguous components and verify the register records them.

## Considerations
Debt records should be actionable and non-punitive. The goal is to turn unknowns into known unknowns.
close_reason: Added source-backed .seal/debt.yaml register, validation/reference checks, docs, and focused fixture coverage; npm test and bd dep cycles passed.

title: Define non-expert personas and guided jobs-to-be-done
description:
## Background
The plugin needs to serve planners, founders, product builders, and developers who may not know systems-engineering terminology but still need rigorous plans, traceability, and release discipline.

## Technical Approach
Write the primary personas, their inputs, their fears, their expected outputs, and the exact guided jobs SEAL should support. Include one persona that starts from gstack output and one that starts from an existing repo.

## Success Criteria
A checked-in product note names the first personas, top workflows, non-goals, and terminology that should be hidden from or explained to beginners.

## Test Plan
Walk through each persona and verify the first-run command can be described in one sentence using plain language.

## Considerations
Do not let systems-engineering vocabulary become the product surface unless paired with plain-language explanations.
close_reason: Added checked-in personas note with gstack and existing-repo personas, guided jobs, non-goals, beginner-safe terminology, and document test coverage.

title: Validate fully mapped repo coverage
description:
## Background
A project can look rigorous while major files or components remain unmapped. Coverage checks make blind spots visible.

## Technical Approach
Compare repository inventory to .seal/map.yaml and the debt register. Enforce 100 percent classification of non-ignored files, and require every non-generated, non-vendored file to map to a component or explicit unknown gap.

## Success Criteria
Coverage validation reports mapped, unmapped, ignored, generated, vendored, and unknown areas, and launch gates fail when unmapped files are not visible as gaps.

## Test Plan
Use fixture repos with known unmapped files and components.

## Considerations
Fully mapped means nothing is invisible. It does not mean SEAL magically knows every answer.
close_reason: Added file coverage validation for repository inventory versus map/debt artifacts, including unmapped, uncovered, stale, generated, vendored, unknown, and orphan component checks; npm test and bd dep cycles passed.

title: Implement cross-artifact reference validation
description:
## Background
Traceability breaks when IDs are duplicated, missing, or linked with invalid relationship types.

## Technical Approach
Validate all IDs and references across the .seal folder and report dangling refs, duplicate IDs, invalid link types, and unreachable required artifacts.

## Success Criteria
Reference problems are caught before launch reports or impact analysis trust them.

## Test Plan
Run duplicate, dangling, invalid-type, and missing-file fixtures.

## Considerations
Reference validation should run after schema validation so errors are easier to interpret.
close_reason: Wired cross-artifact reference diagnostics into seal-validate and added file-backed duplicate, dangling, invalid-link, and missing-artifact validation coverage; npm test and bd dep cycles passed.

title: Implement schema validation with actionable errors
description:
## Background
Schema validation is the first guardrail against broken artifact generation.

## Technical Approach
Wire the artifact schemas into a validator command that reports file, path, expected shape, actual value, and suggested fix where practical.

## Success Criteria
Invalid structure fails validation with messages a beginner can use.

## Test Plan
Run validator against intentionally malformed fixtures.

## Considerations
Do not expose raw validator internals as the only error message.
close_reason: Added seal-validate CLI, reusable artifact validation diagnostics, malformed fixture coverage, manifest/package/docs wiring, and passing focused, npm, and bead graph checks.

title: Fresh clone RC commands leave CLI source files line-ending dirty
description: Fresh GitHub clone rc.4 exact scenario: after npm ci and running repo map, impact, proof, launch, validate, plan ingest, and validate, git status shows src/cli/*.mjs modified even though blob hashes are unchanged. git diff reports LF-to-CRLF warnings only. Classify as usability blocker/trust-proof blocker because successful verification leaves users with unexpected dirty source files.
close_reason: Pinned JavaScript and module files to LF in .gitattributes; fresh GitHub clone RC workflow now leaves tracked source files clean, with only generated .seal outputs untracked.

title: Promote SEAL v0.1.0 after fresh-install RC validation
description: Promote v0.0.0-rc.5 to v0.1.0 after multiple real external fresh-clone installs pass without new release-blocking, trust-blocking, or onboarding-blocking gaps. Evidence: remote clone repo workflow and remote clone plan workflow both passed npm ci and seal validation.
close_reason: Promoted package and plugin metadata to v0.1.0 after two independent remote fresh-clone installs passed documented repo and plan workflows, plus npm test, npm run smoke:plugin, and bd dep cycles passed locally.

title: Align RC workflow command surface with repo map/plan ingest/proof/launch verbs
description:
## Observation
The RC feedback loop asks real users to install from GitHub and run: repo map, plan ingest, impact, proof, launch, validate. Current README, plugin manifest, and package bin expose longer seal-* commands instead: seal-invoke, seal-inventory, seal-impact, seal-proof-report, seal-launch-report, and seal-validate.

## Classification
usability blocker; docs mismatch

## Risk
A fresh user following the RC scenario cannot discover whether the listed verbs are conceptual steps, Codex skill prompts, or actual commands. That makes external WATCH runs noisy and can create false confidence if maintainers only test the internal seal-* command names.

## Success Criteria
Document or implement a single beginner-safe command path that maps the RC verbs to executable package commands. Fresh-clone verification must run the documented repo map, plan ingest, impact, proof, launch, and validate scenario successfully.

## Test Plan
Run npm test, npm run smoke:plugin, and a fresh clone workflow that follows the documented RC command path.
close_reason: Added the public seal wrapper command, documented repo map / plan ingest / impact / proof / launch / validate, and covered the RC workflow with regression tests.
